title: Pheochromocytoma

patient.name: Elena Rodriguez
patient.persona: ER nurse, frustrated by 'panic attacks' that don't respond to treatment

chief_complaint: I keep having these terrifying episodes. My heart pounds, my head feels like it's going to explode, I sweat through my clothes, and I feel like I'm going to die. They come out of nowhere and last about 15 minutes. It's not anxiety - I know what panic attacks feel like.

physical_exam.Cardiac: Tachycardia, regular, forceful apical impulse. No murmurs.
physical_exam.Skin: Pale, cool, clammy during episode. Café-au-lait spots on trunk (6 spots >1.5cm - neurofibromatosis type 1).
physical_exam.Neurologic: Tremor in both hands. No focal deficits.
physical_exam.Fundoscopic: Grade 2 hypertensive retinopathy (AV nicking, copper wiring)

hidden_data.plasma_metanephrines: Metanephrine 4.5 nmol/L (normal <0.5), Normetanephrine 8.2 nmol/L (normal <0.9) - SEVERELY elevated ×9-10 upper limit
hidden_data.urinary_metanephrines: 24h urinary metanephrines: Metanephrine 3,200 mcg/24h (normal <400), Normetanephrine 5,800 mcg/24h (normal <900)
hidden_data.ct_adrenal: Right adrenal mass 4.5cm × 3.8cm, heterogeneous enhancement, areas of cystic degeneration. Hounsfield units >25 on non-contrast CT. Left adrenal normal.
hidden_data.mri_adrenal: Right adrenal mass with high signal intensity on T2-weighted imaging (classic 'light bulb' sign). No invasion of surrounding structures.
hidden_data.mibg_scan: I-123 MIBG scan: Intense uptake in right adrenal mass. No extra-adrenal uptake. No metastases.
hidden_data.genetic_testing: RET proto-oncogene: Negative. VHL gene: Negative. NF1: Clinically diagnosed (café-au-lait spots + axillary freckling). SDHB/SDHD: Pending.
hidden_data.ecg: Sinus tachycardia 104 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy with strain pattern.
hidden_data.echo: Moderate concentric LVH. EF 65%. No regional wall motion abnormalities.

correct_diagnosis: Pheochromocytoma of Right Adrenal Gland in Neurofibromatosis Type 1 (NF1)
key_learning_points: Pheochromocytoma = catecholamine-secreting tumor of adrenal chromaffin cells. Paraganglioma = extra-adrenal chromaffin cell tumor
Classic triad: Paroxysmal hypertension + palpitations + headache + diaphoresis (Rule of 10s: 10% bilateral, 10% malignant, 10% extra-adrenal, 10% familial)
Diagnosis: Plasma free metanephrines (sensitivity 97%) or 24h urinary fractionated metanephrines. Levels >3-4× upper limit are diagnostic
Imaging: CT/MRI for localization. I-123 MIBG scan for functional confirmation, metastasis detection, and treatment planning
Pre-operative alpha-blockade (Phenoxybenzamine or Doxazosin) for 10-14 days BEFORE beta-blockade to prevent hypertensive crisis from unopposed alpha stimulation

patient_responses.episodes: They happen 2-3 times a week. Suddenly I feel this wave of heat, my heart goes crazy, my head pounds so hard I can feel it in my ears. I'm drenched in sweat. Sometimes it happens after I bend over or lift something, sometimes just sitting still.
patient_responses.anxiety: Doctors keep telling me it's anxiety but I know my body. Anxiety meds don't help. These episodes are different - they're physical, not psychological.
patient_responses.other_symptoms: Between episodes I feel mostly fine, just tired. I've lost 15 pounds in 3 months without trying. I'm constipated which is new for me. Sometimes my vision blurs.
patient_responses.family_history: My father had 'some kind of adrenal tumor' removed in his 40s. He also had those brown spots on his skin like I do. He died of a stroke at 52.
patient_responses.medications: I take lisinopril 40mg, amlodipine 10mg, and hydrochlorothiazide for my blood pressure but it's never controlled. I also take sertraline for 'anxiety' but it doesn't help the episodes.

hints.history: Paroxysmal triad of headache + palpitations + sweating in a patient with resistant HTN and NF1 stigmata. Ask about family history of MEN2, NF1, VHL, or SDH mutations.
hints.examination: Look for neurocutaneous syndromes (NF1: café-au-lait spots, neurofibromas, Lisch nodules). Hypertensive retinopathy indicates chronic uncontrolled HTN.
hints.labs: Plasma free metanephrines is the best initial test (highest sensitivity). 24h urinary metanephrines for confirmation. Genetic testing if <50 years old.
hints.imaging: CT adrenal (preferred initial imaging). MRI if CT inconclusive or radiation concern. I-123 MIBG scan for functional confirmation and metastasis evaluation.

feedback.correct_message: Outstanding diagnosis! You correctly identified pheochromocytoma based on paroxysmal HTN triad, severely elevated plasma metanephrines (>9× ULN), adrenal mass on imaging, and MIBG confirmation. Recognizing the NF1 connection (café-au-lait spots) is excellent. Alpha-blockade must precede beta-blockade to prevent hypertensive crisis. Adrenalectomy is curative.
feedback.partially_correct_message: Good recognition of an adrenal mass! However, the severely elevated metanephrines (>9× ULN) and classic paroxysmal symptoms confirm this is a functional pheochromocytoma, not a non-functional adrenal incidentaloma. Pre-operative alpha-blockade is mandatory before surgery to prevent intraoperative hypertensive crisis.
feedback.incorrect_message: The paroxysmal triad (headache, palpitations, sweating) with resistant HTN, café-au-lait spots (NF1), metanephrines 9× ULN, and 4.5cm adrenal mass is classic pheochromocytoma. This is NOT anxiety - it's a catecholamine-secreting tumor. Alpha-blockade must be started immediately (Phenoxybenzamine 10mg BID, titrate up) followed by beta-blockade. Surgical resection is curative but requires meticulous pre-operative preparation.
clinical_pearls: NEVER start beta-blocker before alpha-blocker in pheochromocytoma! This causes unopposed alpha stimulation → severe vasoconstriction → hypertensive crisis
Plasma free metanephrines sensitivity 97% - a normal result essentially rules out pheochromocytoma
All patients diagnosed <50 years should be offered genetic testing. 40% have a germline mutation (RET, VHL, NF1, SDHx)

plasma_metanephrines.comment: Metanephrine 4.5 and Normetanephrine 8.2 (>9× ULN) are diagnostic for pheochromocytoma

ct_adrenal.comment: 4.5cm right adrenal mass with HU>25 on non-contrast CT. Heterogeneous with cystic degeneration typical of pheochromocytoma

mibg_scan.comment: Confirms functional neuroendocrine tumor. No extra-adrenal disease or metastases - localized, resectable

genetic_testing.comment: NF1 clinically diagnosed (café-au-lait spots). RET and VHL negative. SDHx pending

echo.comment: LVH from chronic catecholamine excess. Assess cardiac function pre-operatively

feedback.attending_note: Doctor, start Phenoxybenzamine 10mg PO BID, titrate every 2-3 days by 10-20mg/day to target BP <130/80 and HR <90. Only after adequate alpha-blockade (10-14 days): add Propranolol 20mg TID. High-sodium diet to expand plasma volume (catecholamine-induced volume contraction). IVF 1-2L NS day before surgery. Refer to endocrine surgeon for laparoscopic adrenalectomy. Monitor for post-operative hypotension (needs IVF ± vasopressors) and hypoglycemia (rebound hyperinsulinism). Genetic counseling for NF1 and family screening.
